expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: §15 Decision Coverage Checklist contains all 22 rows (A1-A14, B1-B8), each with a Resolved/Assumed/N/A status and a substantive note citing specific sections. All 15 document sections are present and filled.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: §11 Assumptions and §12 Accepted Compromises are both formatted as full markdown tables. Every row in §12 has a 'Principle bent' column (e.g. C1: "Principle 5 (design for failure)", C2: "Principle 8 (measure, don't guess)") and a 'Revisit trigger' column (e.g. C2: "If query p95 > 2 s for large workspaces, or if user count exceeds 1 M").

expectations.text: The idempotency mechanism guarantees one digest per user per week even under a concurrent double-run of the job — it relies on a database-enforced dedup (e.g. a unique key on user + week), not a check-then-send that has a race window. (Correctness trap: an application-level 'already sent?' check is not concurrency-safe.)
expectations.evidence: D2 states: "The check-then-insert uses a unique index + INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (or equivalent advisory-lock pattern) to prevent race conditions if the scheduler fires twice." §4 data model shows: "CONSTRAINT digest_deliveries_user_week_unique UNIQUE (user_id, week_start_date)". The DB constraint is explicitly named as the backstop.

expectations.text: The design handles the email-provider failure rule correctly: retries are time-bounded, and on continued failure the digest is dropped for the week rather than delayed — and the design explicitly ties this to the blueprint's 'a late digest is worse than a skipped one' rule.
expectations.evidence: D5: "After exhaustion, the job is moved to the dead queue and the digest is skipped for that week. No compensating send is attempted later." C1 accepted compromise states: "Blueprint explicitly states 'a digest that arrives late is worse than one skipped.' Blueprint governs." Retries are capped at 3 (time-bounded via Sidekiq exponential backoff).

expectations.text: Per-user-timezone send scheduling is designed concretely (how 8am-local across many timezones becomes scheduled sends), and the design handles the blueprint's 'job runs late' deviation without corrupting the seven-day content window.
expectations.evidence: D7: "One Sidekiq cron entry fires DigestSchedulerJob every minute. The job computes which users have their 8 AM Monday local-time falling within a ±30-second window of the current UTC instant." For the late-job deviation, §7 failure table states: "Scheduler delayed / starts late — week_start_date computed from user's timezone at run time; content window is unchanged. Send time may slip; blueprint explicitly accepts this."

expectations.text: The design specifies that no email is sent when a user has no relevant activity, and correctly handles the mid-week new-user partial window — both blueprint rules.
expectations.evidence: No-activity rule is handled (§7: "User has no activity — Job writes status='skipped_no_activity'; no email sent"). However, the mid-week new-user partial window is NOT addressed. The document never specifies that the query window lower bound should be clamped to the user's join date. A13 in §11 only mentions "ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 10" as the highlighted-items query; it does not address the window start for new users. The edge case from the blueprint ("a brand-new user who joined mid-week receives a digest covering only the days since they joined") has no corresponding design element.

expectations.text: The unsubscribe link is designed to be unguessable and scoped to the issuing account (a signed or random token, not a sequential identifier), addressing the adversarial scenario; and digest content is scoped to only what each user is permitted to see.
expectations.evidence: D6: token is "a unique, opaque, per-user value stored in users.digest_unsubscribe_token" generated using "SecureRandom.urlsafe_base64(48) (64-char token, 288 bits of entropy — not guessable)". §8: "Activity query must filter by workspace membership using the same permission check used elsewhere in the application" via "WorkspacePermission.visible_items_for(user)" — described as "a hard requirement, not an assumption".

expectations.text: The design names the central scheduling trade-off and resolves it with principle-grounded reasoning, and the digest-assembly query is bounded — the 'up to ten highlighted items, +N more' cap is reflected as a real query limit, not unbounded fetching.
expectations.evidence: D7 names and resolves the scheduling trade-off (per-minute cron vs. one-per-timezone cron vs. UTC batch) with principle citations (Principle 1, Principle 2). §11 A13 explicitly states the bounded query: "The 'highlighted items' sort order (recency) is implemented as ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 10 — Blueprint specifies 'chosen by recency, capped at ten'." This is a real LIMIT, not just prose.
